*A brief history of geodesy* from NASA.[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The history of [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") ([/dʒiːˈɒdɪsi/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English")) began during antiquity and ultimately blossomed during the [Age of Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment").

Many early conceptions of the Earth [held it to be flat](/wiki/Flat_earth "Flat earth"), with the heavens being a physical dome spanning over it. Early arguments for a spherical Earth pointed to various more subtle empirical observations, including how lunar eclipses were seen as circular shadows, as well as the fact that [Polaris](/wiki/Polaris "Polaris") is seen lower in the sky as one travels southward.

## Hellenic world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Hellenic world")]

### Initial developments

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Initial developments")]

Though the earliest written mention of a spherical Earth comes from [ancient Greek](/wiki/Ancient_Greek "Ancient Greek") sources, there is no account of how the sphericity of Earth was discovered, or if it was initially simply a guess.[[2]](#cite_note-evans47-2) A plausible explanation given by the historian [Otto E. Neugebauer](/wiki/Otto_E._Neugebauer "Otto E. Neugebauer") is that it was "the experience of travellers that suggested such an explanation for the variation in the observable [altitude](/wiki/Altitude "Altitude") of the pole and the change in the area of circumpolar stars, a change that was quite drastic between [Greek settlements](/wiki/Greek_colonies "Greek colonies")"[[3]](#cite_note-Neugebauer.575–6-3) around the eastern [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea"), particularly those between the [Nile Delta](/wiki/Nile_Delta "Nile Delta") and [Crimea](/wiki/Crimea "Crimea").[[3]](#cite_note-Neugebauer.575–6-3)

Another possible explanation can be traced back to earlier [Phoenician](/wiki/Phoenicia "Phoenicia") sailors. The first [circumnavigation](/wiki/Circumnavigation "Circumnavigation") of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") is described as being undertaken by Phoenician explorers employed by [Egyptian](/wiki/Ancient_Egypt "Ancient Egypt") pharaoh [Necho II](/wiki/Necho_II "Necho II") c. 610–595 BC.[[4]](#cite_note-Friis-4)[[5]](#cite_note-Panchenko-5) In *The Histories*, written 431–425 BC, [Herodotus](/wiki/Herodotus "Herodotus") cast doubt on a report of the Sun observed shining from the north. He stated that the phenomenon was observed by Phoenician explorers during their circumnavigation of Africa ([*The Histories*](https://en.wikisource.org/wiki/History_of_Herodotus/Book_4 "s:History of Herodotus/Book 4"), 4.42) who claimed to have had the Sun on their right when circumnavigating in a clockwise direction. To modern historians, these details confirm the truth of the Phoenicians' report. The historian Dmitri Panchenko hypothesizes that it was the Phoenician circumnavigation of Africa that inspired the theory of a spherical Earth, the earliest mention of which was made by the philosopher [Parmenides](/wiki/Parmenides "Parmenides") in the 5th century BC.[[5]](#cite_note-Panchenko-5) However, nothing certain about their knowledge of geography and navigation has survived; therefore, later researchers have no evidence that they conceived of Earth as spherical.[[4]](#cite_note-Friis-4)

Speculation and theorizing ranged from the flat disc advocated by [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer") to the spherical body reportedly postulated by [Pythagoras](/wiki/Pythagoras "Pythagoras"). [Anaximenes](/wiki/Anaximenes_of_Miletus "Anaximenes of Miletus"), an early Greek philosopher, believed strongly that the Earth was [rectangular](/wiki/Rectangular "Rectangular") in shape. Some early Greek philosophers alluded to a spherical Earth, though with some ambiguity.[[6]](#cite_note-dicks1-6) [Pythagoras](/wiki/Pythagoras "Pythagoras") (6th century BC) was among those said to have originated the idea, but this might reflect the ancient Greek practice of ascribing every discovery to one or another of their ancient wise men.[[2]](#cite_note-evans47-2) Pythagoras was a mathematician, and he supposedly reasoned that the gods would create a perfect figure which to him was a [sphere](/wiki/Sphere "Sphere"), but there is no evidence for this claim.[[7]](#cite_note-7) Some idea of the sphericity of Earth seems to have been known to both [Parmenides](/wiki/Parmenides "Parmenides") and [Empedocles](/wiki/Empedocles "Empedocles") in the 5th century BC,[[8]](#cite_note-kahn53-8) and although the idea cannot reliably be ascribed to Pythagoras,[[9]](#cite_note-9) it might nevertheless have been formulated in the [Pythagorean school](/wiki/Pythagoreanism "Pythagoreanism") in the 5th century BC[[2]](#cite_note-evans47-2)[[8]](#cite_note-kahn53-8) although some disagree.[[10]](#cite_note-10) After the 5th century BC, just a few Greek writers of repute thought the world was anything but round.[[6]](#cite_note-dicks1-6) The Pythagorean idea was supported later by [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle").[[11]](#cite_note-11) Efforts commenced to determine the size of the sphere.

### Plato

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Plato")]

[Plato](/wiki/Plato "Plato") (427–347 BC) travelled to southern [Italy](/wiki/Italy "Italy") to study [Pythagorean mathematics](/wiki/Pythagorean_mathematics "Pythagorean mathematics"). When he returned to [Athens](/wiki/Athens "Athens") and established his school, Plato also taught his students that Earth was a sphere, though he offered no justifications. "My conviction is that the Earth is a round body in the centre of the heavens, and therefore has no need of air or of any similar force to be a support."[[12]](#cite_note-12) If man could soar high above the clouds, Earth would resemble "one of those balls which have leather coverings in twelve pieces, and is decked with various colours, of which the colours used by painters on Earth are in a manner samples."[[13]](#cite_note-13) In [*Timaeus*](/wiki/Timaeus_(dialogue) "Timaeus (dialogue)"), his one work that was available throughout the Middle Ages in Latin, he wrote that the Creator "made the world in the form of a globe, round as from a lathe, having its extremes in every direction equidistant from the centre, the most perfect and the most like itself of all figures",[[14]](#cite_note-14) though the word "world" here refers to the heavens.

### Aristotle

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Aristotle")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/e/eb/20080816eclipsed2.jpg/250px-20080816eclipsed2.jpg)](/wiki/File:20080816eclipsed2.jpg)

Round Earth umbra during the [August 2008 lunar eclipse](/wiki/August_2008_lunar_eclipse "August 2008 lunar eclipse")

[Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") (384–322 BC) was Plato's prize student and "the mind of the school".[[15]](#cite_note-15) Aristotle observed "there are [stars](/wiki/Star "Star") seen in [Egypt](/wiki/Egypt "Egypt") and [...] [Cyprus](/wiki/Cyprus "Cyprus") which are not seen in the northerly regions". Since this could only happen on a curved surface, he too believed Earth was a sphere "of no great size, for otherwise the effect of so slight a change of place would not be quickly apparent".[[16]](#cite_note-16)

Aristotle reported the circumference of the Earth (which is actually slightly over 40,000 km or 24,000 miles) to be 400,000 [stadia](/wiki/Stadion_(unit) "Stadion (unit)") (45,000 miles or 74,000 km).[[17]](#cite_note-17)

Aristotle provided physical and observational arguments supporting the idea of a spherical Earth:

* Every portion of Earth tends toward the centre until by compression and convergence they form a sphere.[[18]](#cite_note-18)
* Travelers going south see southern constellations rise higher above the horizon.
* The shadow of Earth on the Moon during a [lunar eclipse](/wiki/Lunar_eclipse "Lunar eclipse") is round.[[19]](#cite_note-19)

The concepts of symmetry, equilibrium and cyclic repetition permeated Aristotle's work. In his [*Meteorology*](/wiki/Meteorology_(Aristotle) "Meteorology (Aristotle)") he divided the world into five climatic zones: two temperate areas separated by a torrid zone near the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), and two cold inhospitable regions, "one near our upper or northern pole and the other near the [...] southern pole", both impenetrable and girdled with ice.[[20]](#cite_note-20) Although no humans could survive in the frigid zones, inhabitants in the southern temperate regions could exist.

[Aristotle's theory of natural place](/wiki/Aristotelian_physics#Natural_place "Aristotelian physics") relied on a spherical Earth to explain why [heavy things](/wiki/Earth_(classical_element) "Earth (classical element)") go down (toward what Aristotle believed was the center of the Universe), and things like [air](/wiki/Air_(classical_element) "Air (classical element)") and [fire](/wiki/Fire_(classical_element) "Fire (classical element)") go up. In this [geocentric model](/wiki/Geocentric_model "Geocentric model"), the structure of the universe was believed to be a series of perfect spheres. The Sun, Moon, planets and fixed stars were believed to move on [celestial spheres](/wiki/Celestial_spheres "Celestial spheres") around a stationary Earth.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Though Aristotle's theory of physics survived in the Christian world for many centuries, his geocentric model was eventually superseded by the [heliocentric model](/wiki/Heliocentric_model "Heliocentric model") as an explanation of the [Solar System](/wiki/Solar_System "Solar System"), while [atomic theory](/wiki/Atomic_theory "Atomic theory") took the place of the [classical elements](/wiki/Classical_element "Classical element") such as earth, water, air, fire, and [aether](/wiki/Aether_(classical_element) "Aether (classical element)").[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Archimedes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Archimedes")]

[Archimedes](/wiki/Archimedes "Archimedes") (c. 287 – c. 212 BC) gave an upper bound for the circumference of the Earth.

In proposition 2 of the First Book of his treatise *[On Floating Bodies](/wiki/On_Floating_Bodies "On Floating Bodies")*, Archimedes demonstrates that "The surface of any fluid at rest is the surface of a sphere whose centre is the same as that of the Earth."[[21]](#cite_note-21) Subsequently, in propositions 8 and 9 of the same work, he assumes the result of proposition 2 that Earth is a sphere and that the surface of a fluid on it is a sphere centered on the center of Earth.[[22]](#cite_note-22)

### Eratosthenes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Eratosthenes")]

[Eratosthenes](/wiki/Eratosthenes "Eratosthenes") (276–194 BC), a [Hellenistic astronomer](/wiki/Hellenistic_astronomy "Hellenistic astronomy") from what is now [Cyrene, Libya](/wiki/Cyrene,_Libya "Cyrene, Libya"), working in [Alexandria, Egypt](/wiki/Alexandria,_Egypt "Alexandria, Egypt"), estimated [Earth](/wiki/Earth "Earth")'s circumference around 240 BC, computing a value of 252,000 *[stades](/wiki/Ancient_Greek_units_of_measurement "Ancient Greek units of measurement")*. The length that Eratosthenes intended for a "stade" is not known, but his figure only has an error of around one to five percent.[[23]](#cite_note-russo273277-23) Assuming a value for the stadion between 155 and 160 metres, the error is between −2.4% and +0.8%.[[23]](#cite_note-russo273277-23) Eratosthenes described his technique in a book entitled *On the Measure of the Earth*, which has not been preserved. Eratosthenes could only measure the circumference of Earth by assuming that the [distance to the Sun](/wiki/Distance_to_the_Sun "Distance to the Sun") is so great that the rays of [sunlight](/wiki/Sunlight "Sunlight") are practically [parallel](/wiki/Parallel_(geometry) "Parallel (geometry)").[[24]](#cite_note-24)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/1f/Eratosthenes_measure_of_Earth_circumference.svg/500px-Eratosthenes_measure_of_Earth_circumference.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Eratosthenes_measure_of_Earth_circumference.svg)

Measure of Earth's circumference according to Cleomedes' simplified version, based on the wrong assumption that [Syene](/wiki/Syene "Syene") is on the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer") and on the same meridian as [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria").

Eratosthenes's method to calculate the [Earth's circumference](/wiki/Earth%27s_circumference "Earth's circumference") has been lost; what has been preserved is the simplified version described by [Cleomedes](/wiki/Cleomedes "Cleomedes") to popularise the discovery.[[25]](#cite_note-25) Cleomedes invites his reader to consider two Egyptian cities, [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria") and [Syene](/wiki/Syene "Syene"), modern [Assuan](/wiki/Assuan "Assuan"):

1. Cleomedes assumes that the distance between Syene and Alexandria was 5,000 stadia (a figure that was checked yearly by professional [bematists](/wiki/Bematist "Bematist"), *mensores regii*);[[26]](#cite_note-26)
2. he assumes the simplified hypothesis that Syene was precisely on the [Tropic of Cancer](/wiki/Tropic_of_Cancer "Tropic of Cancer"), saying that at [local noon](/wiki/Solar_noon "Solar noon") on the summer [solstice](/wiki/Solstice "Solstice") the Sun was directly overhead;
3. he assumes the simplified hypothesis that Syene and Alexandria are on the same meridian.

Under the previous assumptions, says Cleomedes, one can measure the Sun's angle of elevation at noon of the summer solstice in Alexandria, by using a vertical rod (a [gnomon](/wiki/Gnomon "Gnomon")) of known length and measuring the length of its shadow on the ground; it is then possible to calculate the angle of the Sun's rays, which he claims to be about 7.2°, or 1/50th the circumference of a circle. Taking the Earth as spherical, the Earth's circumference would be fifty times the distance between Alexandria and Syene, that is 250,000 stadia. Since 1 Egyptian stadium is equal to 157.5 metres, the result is 39,375 km, which is 1.4% less than the real number, 39,941 km.

Eratosthenes's method was actually more complicated, as stated by the same Cleomedes, whose purpose was to present a simplified version of the one described in Eratosthenes's book. The method was based on several [surveying](/wiki/Surveying "Surveying") trips conducted by professional bematists, whose job was to precisely measure the extent of the territory of Egypt for agricultural and taxation-related purposes.[[23]](#cite_note-russo273277-23) Furthermore, the fact that Eratosthenes's measure corresponds precisely to 252,000 stadia might be intentional, since it is a number that can be divided by all natural numbers from 1 to 10: some historians believe that Eratosthenes changed from the 250,000 value written by Cleomedes to this new value to simplify calculations;[[27]](#cite_note-27) other historians of science, on the other side, believe that Eratosthenes introduced a new length unit based on the length of the meridian, as stated by Pliny, who writes about the stadion "according to Eratosthenes's ratio".[[23]](#cite_note-russo273277-23)[[28]](#cite_note-28)

1,700 years after Eratosthenes, [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") studied Eratosthenes's findings before sailing west for the Indies. However, ultimately he rejected Eratosthenes in favour of other maps and arguments that interpreted Earth's circumference to be a third smaller than it really is. If, instead, Columbus had accepted Eratosthenes's findings, he might have never gone west, since he did not have the supplies or funding needed for the much longer eight-thousand-plus mile voyage.[[29]](#cite_note-29)

### Seleucus of Seleucia

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Seleucus of Seleucia")]

[Seleucus of Seleucia](/wiki/Seleucus_of_Seleucia "Seleucus of Seleucia") (c. 190 BC), who lived in the city of [Seleucia](/wiki/Seleucia "Seleucia") in [Mesopotamia](/wiki/Mesopotamia "Mesopotamia"), wrote that Earth is spherical (and actually orbits the [Sun](/wiki/Sun "Sun"), influenced by the [heliocentric theory](/wiki/Heliocentrism "Heliocentrism") of [Aristarchus of Samos](/wiki/Aristarchus_of_Samos "Aristarchus of Samos")).

### Posidonius

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Posidonius")]

A parallel later ancient measurement of the size of the Earth was made by another Greek scholar, [Posidonius](/wiki/Posidonius "Posidonius") (c. 135 – 51 BC), using a similar method as Eratosthenes. Instead of observing the Sun, he noted that the star [Canopus](/wiki/Canopus "Canopus") was hidden from view in most parts of Greece but that it just grazed the horizon at Rhodes. Posidonius is supposed to have measured the angular elevation of Canopus at Alexandria and determined that the angle was 1/48 of a circle. He used a distance from Alexandria to Rhodes, 5000 stadia, and so he computed the Earth's circumference in stadia as 48 × 5000 = 240,000.[[30]](#cite_note-30) Some scholars see these results as luckily semi-accurate due to cancellation of errors. But since the Canopus observations are both mistaken by over a degree, the "experiment" may be not much more than a recycling of Eratosthenes's numbers, while altering 1/50 to the correct 1/48 of a circle. Later, either he or a follower appears to have altered the base distance to agree with Eratosthenes's Alexandria-to-Rhodes figure of 3750 stadia, since Posidonius' final circumference was 180,000 stadia, which equals 48 × 3750 stadia.[[31]](#cite_note-31) The 180,000 stadia circumference of Posidonius is close to that which results from another method of measuring the Earth, by timing ocean sunsets from different heights, a method which is inaccurate due to horizontal [atmospheric refraction](/wiki/Atmospheric_refraction "Atmospheric refraction"). Posidonius furthermore expressed the distance of the Sun in Earth radii.

The above-mentioned larger and smaller sizes of the Earth were those used by later Roman author [Claudius Ptolemy](/wiki/Claudius_Ptolemy "Claudius Ptolemy") at different times: 252,000 stadia in his *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")* and 180,000 stadia in his later *[Geographia](/wiki/Geography_(Ptolemy) "Geography (Ptolemy)")*. His mid-career conversion resulted in the latter work's systematic exaggeration of degree longitudes in the Mediterranean by a factor close to the ratio of the two seriously differing sizes discussed here, which indicates that the conventional size of the Earth was what changed, not the stadion.[[32]](#cite_note-32)

## Roman Empire

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Roman Empire")]

The idea of a spherical Earth slowly spread across the globe, and ultimately became the adopted view in all major astronomical traditions.[[33]](#cite_note-Krüger:_Roman_and_medieval_continuation-33)[[34]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_of_Islam:_Astronomy-34)[[35]](#cite_note-Pingree:_Indian_adoption-35)[[36]](#cite_note-Martzloff,_Cullen:_Chinese_adoption-36)

In the West, the idea came to the Romans through the lengthy process of cross-fertilization with [Hellenistic civilization](/wiki/Hellenistic_civilization "Hellenistic civilization"). Many Roman authors such as [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero") and [Pliny](/wiki/Pliny_the_Elder "Pliny the Elder") refer in their works to the rotundity of Earth as a matter of course.[[37]](#cite_note-Reinhard_Krüger-37) Pliny also considered the possibility of an imperfect sphere "shaped like a pinecone".[[38]](#cite_note-38)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/c7/Curvatura_2.gif/250px-Curvatura_2.gif)](/wiki/File:Curvatura_2.gif)

When a ship is at the horizon, its [lower part is obscured](/wiki/Hull-down "Hull-down") by Earth's curvature. This was one of the first arguments favouring a round-Earth model.[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Strabo

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: Strabo")]

It has been suggested that seafarers probably provided the first observational evidence that Earth was not flat, based on observations of the [horizon](/wiki/Horizon "Horizon"). This argument was put forward by the geographer [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") (c. 64 BC – 24 AD), who suggested that the spherical shape of Earth was probably known to seafarers around the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") since at least the time of [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer"),[[39]](#cite_note-strabo-39) citing a line from the *[Odyssey](/wiki/Odyssey "Odyssey")*[[40]](#cite_note-40) as indicating that the poet [Homer](/wiki/Homer "Homer") knew of this as early as the 7th or 8th century BC. [Strabo](/wiki/Strabo "Strabo") cited various phenomena observed at sea as suggesting that Earth was spherical. He observed that elevated lights or areas of land were visible to sailors at greater distances than those less elevated, and stated that the curvature of the sea was obviously responsible for this.[[41]](#cite_note-41)

### Claudius Ptolemy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Claudius Ptolemy")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f0/Claudius_Ptolemy-_The_World.jpg/500px-Claudius_Ptolemy-_The_World.jpg)](/wiki/File:Claudius_Ptolemy-_The_World.jpg)

A printed map from the 15th century depicting Ptolemy's description of the *[Ecumene](/wiki/Ecumene "Ecumene")* (the inhabited world).  
 (1482, by [Nicolaus Germanus](/wiki/Nicolaus_Germanus "Nicolaus Germanus"))

[Claudius Ptolemy](/wiki/Claudius_Ptolemy "Claudius Ptolemy") (90–168 AD) lived in [Alexandria](/wiki/Alexandria "Alexandria"), the centre of scholarship in the 2nd century. In the *[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")*, which remained the standard work of astronomy for 1,400 years, he advanced many arguments for the spherical nature of Earth. Among them was the observation that when a ship is sailing towards [mountains](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain"), observers note these seem to rise from the sea, indicating that they were hidden by the curved surface of the sea. He also gives separate arguments that Earth is curved north–south and that it is curved east–west.[[42]](#cite_note-42)

He compiled an eight-volume *[Geographia](/wiki/Geographia_(Ptolemy) "Geographia (Ptolemy)")* covering what was known about Earth. The first part of the *Geographia* is a discussion of the data and of the methods he used. As with the model of the Solar System in the *Almagest*, Ptolemy put all this information into a grand scheme. He assigned [coordinates](/wiki/Coordinate "Coordinate") to all the places and geographic features he knew, in a [grid](/wiki/Grid_(spatial_index) "Grid (spatial index)") that spanned the globe (although most of this has been lost). [Latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") was measured from the [equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator"), as it is today, but Ptolemy preferred to express it as the length of the longest day rather than [degrees of arc](/wiki/Degree_(angle) "Degree (angle)") (the length of the [midsummer](/wiki/Midsummer "Midsummer") day increases from 12h to 24h as you go from the equator to the [polar circle](/wiki/Polar_circle "Polar circle")). He put the [meridian](/wiki/Meridian_(geography) "Meridian (geography)") of 0 [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") at the most western land he knew, the [Canary Islands](/wiki/Canary_Islands "Canary Islands").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/83/A_modern_reconstruction_of_Claudius_Ptolemy%27s_map_of_Europe_and_North_Africa_by_Kotsanas_Museum_of_Ancient_Greek_Technology.jpg/250px-A_modern_reconstruction_of_Claudius_Ptolemy%27s_map_of_Europe_and_North_Africa_by_Kotsanas_Museum_of_Ancient_Greek_Technology.jpg)](/wiki/File:A_modern_reconstruction_of_Claudius_Ptolemy%27s_map_of_Europe_and_North_Africa_by_Kotsanas_Museum_of_Ancient_Greek_Technology.jpg)

A modern reconstruction of [Claudius Ptolemy's](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") map of [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") and [North Africa](/wiki/North_Africa "North Africa") by Kostas Kotsanas, in [Kotsanas Museum of Ancient Greek Technology](/wiki/Museum_of_Ancient_Greek_Technology "Museum of Ancient Greek Technology"), Athens.

*Geographia* indicated the countries of "[Serica](/wiki/Serica "Serica")" and "Sinae" ([China](/wiki/China "China")) at the extreme right, beyond the island of "Taprobane" ([Sri Lanka](/wiki/Sri_Lanka "Sri Lanka"), oversized) and the "Aurea Chersonesus" ([Southeast Asian peninsula](/wiki/Southeast_Asia "Southeast Asia")).

Ptolemy also devised and provided instructions on how to make maps both of the whole inhabited world (*oikoumenè*) and of the Roman provinces. In the second part of the *Geographia*, he provided the necessary [topographic](/wiki/Topographic "Topographic") lists, and captions for the maps. His *oikoumenè* spanned 180 degrees of longitude from the Canary Islands in the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean") to [China](/wiki/China "China"), and about 81 degrees of latitude from the Arctic to the [East Indies](/wiki/East_Indies "East Indies") and deep into [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa"). Ptolemy was well aware that he knew about only a quarter of the globe.

### Late Antiquity

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: Late Antiquity")]

Knowledge of the spherical shape of Earth was received in scholarship of [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity") as a matter of course, in both [Neoplatonism](/wiki/Neoplatonism "Neoplatonism") and [Early Christianity](/wiki/Early_Christianity "Early Christianity"). [Calcidius](/wiki/Calcidius "Calcidius")'s fourth-century [Latin](/wiki/Latin_language "Latin language") commentary on and translation of Plato's *Timaeus*, which was one of the few examples of Greek scientific thought that was known in the Early Middle Ages in Western Europe, discussed [Hipparchus](/wiki/Hipparchus "Hipparchus")'s use of the geometrical circumstances of eclipses in *[On Sizes and Distances](/wiki/On_Sizes_and_Distances_(Hipparchus) "On Sizes and Distances (Hipparchus)")* to compute the relative diameters of the Sun, Earth, and Moon.[[43]](#cite_note-43)[[44]](#cite_note-44)

Theological doubt informed by the [flat Earth](/wiki/Flat_Earth "Flat Earth") model implied in the [Hebrew Bible](/wiki/Hebrew_Bible "Hebrew Bible") inspired some early Christian scholars such as [Lactantius](/wiki/Lactantius "Lactantius"), [John Chrysostom](/wiki/John_Chrysostom "John Chrysostom") and [Athanasius of Alexandria](/wiki/Athanasius_of_Alexandria "Athanasius of Alexandria"), but this remained an eccentric current. Learned Christian authors such as [Basil of Caesarea](/wiki/Basil_of_Caesarea "Basil of Caesarea"), [Ambrose](/wiki/Ambrose "Ambrose") and [Augustine of Hippo](/wiki/Augustine_of_Hippo "Augustine of Hippo") were clearly aware of the sphericity of Earth. "Flat Earthism" lingered longest in [Syriac Christianity](/wiki/Syriac_Christianity "Syriac Christianity"), which tradition laid greater importance on a literalist interpretation of the Old Testament. Authors from that tradition, such as [Cosmas Indicopleustes](/wiki/Cosmas_Indicopleustes "Cosmas Indicopleustes"), presented Earth as flat as late as in the 6th century. This last remnant of the ancient model of the cosmos disappeared during the 7th century. From the 8th century and the beginning [medieval period](/wiki/Early_Middle_Ages "Early Middle Ages"), "no cosmographer worthy of note has called into question the sphericity of the Earth".[[45]](#cite_note-45)

Such widely read encyclopedists as [Macrobius](/wiki/Macrobius "Macrobius") and [Martianus Capella](/wiki/Martianus_Capella "Martianus Capella") (both 5th century AD) discussed the circumference of the sphere of the Earth, its central position in the universe, the difference of the [seasons](/wiki/Season "Season") in [Northern](/wiki/Northern_Hemisphere "Northern Hemisphere") and [Southern Hemispheres](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), and many other geographical details.[[46]](#cite_note-Macrobius-46) In his commentary on [Cicero](/wiki/Cicero "Cicero")'s *[Dream of Scipio](/wiki/Dream_of_Scipio "Dream of Scipio")*, Macrobius described the Earth as a globe of insignificant size in comparison to the remainder of the cosmos.[[46]](#cite_note-Macrobius-46)

## Ancient India

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Ancient India")]

Further information: [Indian astronomy](/wiki/Indian_astronomy "Indian astronomy") and [Indian mathematics](/wiki/Indian_mathematics "Indian mathematics")

While the textual evidence has not survived, the precision of the constants used in pre-Greek *[Vedanga](/wiki/Vedanga "Vedanga")* models, and the model's accuracy in predicting the Moon and Sun's motion for Vedic rituals, probably came from direct astronomical observations. The cosmographic theories[*[clarification needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Please_clarify "Wikipedia:Please clarify")*] and assumptions in ancient India likely developed independently and in parallel, but these were influenced by some unknown quantitative Greek astronomy text in the medieval era.[[47]](#cite_note-47)[[48]](#cite_note-48)

With the spread of [Hellenistic culture](/wiki/Hellenistic_culture "Hellenistic culture") in the east, [Hellenistic astronomy](/wiki/Hellenistic_astronomy "Hellenistic astronomy") filtered eastwards to [ancient India](/wiki/Ancient_India "Ancient India") where its profound influence became apparent in the early centuries AD.[[49]](#cite_note-Pingree_(1978),_533,_554f.-49) The Greek concept of an Earth surrounded by the spheres of the planets and that of the fixed stars, vehemently supported by astronomers like [Varāhamihira](/wiki/Var%C4%81hamihira "Varāhamihira") and [Brahmagupta](/wiki/Brahmagupta "Brahmagupta"), strengthened the astronomical principles. Some ideas were found possible to preserve, although in altered form.[[49]](#cite_note-Pingree_(1978),_533,_554f.-49)[[50]](#cite_note-50)

### Aryabhata

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Aryabhata")]

The Indian astronomer and mathematician [Aryabhata](/wiki/Aryabhata "Aryabhata") (476–550 CE) was a pioneer of mathematical astronomy on the subcontinent. He describes the Earth as being spherical and says that it rotates on its axis, among other places in his [Sanskrit](/wiki/Sanskrit "Sanskrit") magnum opus, *[Āryabhaṭīya](/wiki/%C4%80ryabha%E1%B9%AD%C4%ABya "Āryabhaṭīya")*. *Aryabhatiya* is divided into four sections: *Gitika*, *Ganitha* ("mathematics"), *Kalakriya* ("reckoning of time") and *Gola* ("[celestial sphere](/wiki/Celestial_sphere "Celestial sphere")"). The discovery that the Earth rotates on its own axis from west to east is described in *Aryabhatiya* (Gitika 3,6; Kalakriya 5; Gola 9,10).[[51]](#cite_note-autogenerated51-51) For example, he explained the apparent motion of heavenly bodies as only an illusion (Gola 9), with the following simile:

:   Just as a passenger in a boat moving downstream sees the stationary (trees on the river banks) as traversing upstream, so does an observer on earth see the fixed stars as moving towards the west at exactly the same speed (at which the earth moves from west to east.)

In the *Aryabhatiya*, Aryabhata also estimates the circumference of the Earth. He gives this as 4967 [Yojana](/wiki/Yojana "Yojana") and its diameter as 1581 1⁄24 yojana. The length of a Yojana [varies considerably between sources (3.5 - 15 km)](/wiki/Yojana#Variations_in_length "Yojana"); if a yojana is taken to be 8 km (4.97097 miles) the formula offers a circumference of 39,736 kilometres (24,691 mi),[[52]](#cite_note-52) close to the current [equatorial](/wiki/Equator "Equator") value of 40,075 km (24,901 mi).[[53]](#cite_note-53)[[54]](#cite_note-54)

## Islamic world

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Islamic world")]

Main article: [Geography and cartography in medieval Islam: Mathematical geography and geodesy](/wiki/Geography_and_cartography_in_medieval_Islam#Mathematical_geography_and_geodesy "Geography and cartography in medieval Islam")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0b/Abu_Reyhan_Biruni-Earth_Circumference.svg/250px-Abu_Reyhan_Biruni-Earth_Circumference.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Abu_Reyhan_Biruni-Earth_Circumference.svg)

Diagram illustrating a method proposed and used by [Al-Biruni](/wiki/Al-Biruni "Al-Biruni") (973–1048) to estimate the radius and circumference of the Earth

[Islamic astronomy](/wiki/Astronomy_in_medieval_Islam "Astronomy in medieval Islam") was developed on the basis of a spherical Earth inherited from [Hellenistic astronomy](/wiki/Hellenistic_astronomy "Hellenistic astronomy").[[34]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_of_Islam:_Astronomy-34) The Islamic theoretical framework largely relied on the fundamental contributions of [Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle") (*[De caelo](/wiki/De_caelo "De caelo")*) and [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") (*[Almagest](/wiki/Almagest "Almagest")*), both of whom worked from the premise that Earth was spherical and at the centre of the universe ([geocentric model](/wiki/Geocentric_model "Geocentric model")).[[34]](#cite_note-Encyclopaedia_of_Islam:_Astronomy-34)

Early Islamic scholars recognized Earth's sphericity,[[55]](#cite_note-55) leading [Muslim mathematicians](/wiki/Islamic_mathematics "Islamic mathematics") to develop [spherical trigonometry](/wiki/Spherical_trigonometry "Spherical trigonometry")[[56]](#cite_note-David_A._King_1993-56) in order to further mensuration and to calculate the distance and direction from any given point on Earth to [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"). This determined the *[Qibla](/wiki/Qibla "Qibla")*, or Muslim direction of prayer.

### Al-Ma'mun

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: Al-Ma'mun")]

Around 830 CE, [Caliph](/wiki/Caliph "Caliph") [al-Ma'mun](/wiki/Al-Ma%27mun "Al-Ma'mun") commissioned a group of [Muslim astronomers](/wiki/Islamic_astronomy "Islamic astronomy") and [geographers](/wiki/Islamic_geography "Islamic geography") to measure the distance from Tadmur ([Palmyra](/wiki/Palmyra "Palmyra")) to [Raqqa](/wiki/Raqqa "Raqqa") in modern Syria. To determine the length of one degree of [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude"), by using a rope to measure the distance travelled due north or south ([meridian arc](/wiki/Meridian_arc "Meridian arc")) on flat desert land until they reached a place where the altitude of the North Pole had changed by one degree.

[Al-Ma'mun's arc measurement](/wiki/Al-Ma%27mun%27s_arc_measurement "Al-Ma'mun's arc measurement") result is described in different sources as 66 2/3 miles, 56.5 miles, and 56 miles. The figure [Alfraganus](/wiki/Alfraganus "Alfraganus") used based on these measurements was 56 2/3 miles, giving an Earth circumference of 20,400 miles (32,830 km).[[57]](#cite_note-57) 662⁄3 miles results in a calculated planetary circumference of 24,000 miles (39,000 km).[[58]](#cite_note-58)[[59]](#cite_note-59)

Another estimate given by his astronomers was 562⁄3 Arabic miles (111.8 km) per degree, which corresponds to a circumference of 40,248 km, very close to the currently modern values of 111.3 km per degree and 40,068 km circumference, respectively.[[60]](#cite_note-60)[*[contradictory](/wiki/Category:All_self-contradictory_articles "Category:All self-contradictory articles")*]

### Ibn Hazm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Ibn Hazm")]

[Andalusian](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") [polymath](/wiki/Polymath "Polymath") [Ibn Hazm](/wiki/Ibn_Hazm "Ibn Hazm") gave a concise proof of Earth's sphericity: at any given time, there is a point on the Earth where the Sun is directly overhead (which moves throughout the day and throughout the year).[[61]](#cite_note-61)

### Al-Farghānī

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Al-Farghānī")]

[Al-Farghānī](/wiki/Ahmad_ibn_Muhammad_ibn_Kath%C4%ABr_al-Fargh%C4%81n%C4%AB "Ahmad ibn Muhammad ibn Kathīr al-Farghānī") (Latinized as Alfraganus) was a Persian astronomer of the 9th century involved in measuring the diameter of Earth, and commissioned by Al-Ma'mun. His estimate given above for a degree (562⁄3 Arabic miles) was much more accurate than the 602⁄3 Roman miles (89.7 km) given by Ptolemy. [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus") uncritically used Alfraganus's figure as if it were in Roman miles instead of in Arabic miles, in order to prove a smaller size of Earth than that propounded by Ptolemy.[[62]](#cite_note-62)

### Al-Biruni

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Al-Biruni")]

[Abu Rayhan Biruni](/wiki/Abu_Rayhan_Biruni "Abu Rayhan Biruni") (973–1048), in contrast to his predecessors, who measured Earth's circumference by sighting the Sun simultaneously from two different locations, developed a new method of using [trigonometric](/wiki/Trigonometry "Trigonometry") calculations based on the angle between a [plain](/wiki/Plain "Plain") and [mountain](/wiki/Mountain "Mountain") top. This yielded more accurate measurements of Earth's circumference and made it possible for a single person to measure it from a single location.[[63]](#cite_note-63)[[64]](#cite_note-Savizi-64)[[65]](#cite_note-Lumpkin-65) Biruni's method was intended to avoid "walking across hot, dusty deserts", and the idea came to him when he was on top of a tall mountain in India (present day [Pind Dadan Khan](/wiki/Pind_Dadan_Khan "Pind Dadan Khan"), Pakistan).[[65]](#cite_note-Lumpkin-65)

From the top of the mountain, he sighted the dip angle which, along with the mountain's height (which he calculated beforehand), he applied to the [law of sines](/wiki/Law_of_sines "Law of sines") formula to calculate the curvature of the Earth.[[64]](#cite_note-Savizi-64)[[66]](#cite_note-66)[[65]](#cite_note-Lumpkin-65) While this was an ingenious new method, Al-Biruni was not aware of [atmospheric refraction](/wiki/Atmospheric_refraction "Atmospheric refraction"). To get the true dip angle the measured dip angle needs to be corrected by approximately 1/6, meaning that even with perfect measurement his estimate could only have been accurate to within about 20%.[[67]](#cite_note-The_Lost_Art_of_Finding_Our_Way-67)

Biruni also made use of [algebra](/wiki/Algebra "Algebra") to formulate trigonometric equations and used the [astrolabe](/wiki/Astrolabe "Astrolabe") to measure angles.[[68]](#cite_note-Khalili-68)[*[permanent dead link](/wiki/Wikipedia:Link_rot "Wikipedia:Link rot")*][[69]](#cite_note-69)

According to John J. O'Connor and Edmund F. Robertson,

> Important contributions to [geodesy](/wiki/Geodesy "Geodesy") and [geography](/wiki/Islamic_geography "Islamic geography") were also made by Biruni. He introduced techniques to measure Earth and distances on it using [triangulation](/wiki/Triangulation "Triangulation"). He found the radius of Earth to be 6,339.6 kilometres (3,939.2 mi), a value not obtained in the West until the 16th century. His *Masudic canon* contains a table giving the coordinates of six hundred places, almost all of which he had direct knowledge.[[70]](#cite_note-MacTutor-70)

### Al-Zarqali

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Al-Zarqali")]

By 1060, [Andalusi](/wiki/Al-Andalus "Al-Andalus") astronomer [Al-Zarqali](/wiki/Al-Zarqali "Al-Zarqali") corrects geographical data from [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") and [Al-Khwarizmi](/wiki/Al-Khwarizmi "Al-Khwarizmi"), specifically by correcting Ptolemy's estimate of the longitude of the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") from 62 degrees to the correct value of 42 degrees.[[71]](#cite_note-isesco.org.ma-71)

### Jamal-al-Din

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Jamal-al-Din")]

A terrestrial globe (Kura-i-ard) was among the presents sent by the Persian Muslim astronomer [Jamal-al-Din](/wiki/Jamal_ad-Din_(astronomer) "Jamal ad-Din (astronomer)") to [Kublai Khan](/wiki/Kublai_Khan "Kublai Khan")'s [Chinese](/wiki/China "China") court in 1267. It was made of wood on which "seven parts of water are represented in green, three parts of land in white, with rivers, lakes[, et cetera]".[[72]](#cite_note-72) Ho Peng Yoke remarks that "it did not seem to have any general appeal to the Chinese in those days".[[73]](#cite_note-73)

### Applications

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Applications")]

Muslim scholars who held to the spherical Earth theory used it to calculate the distance and direction from any given point on Earth to [Mecca](/wiki/Mecca "Mecca"). [Muslim mathematicians](/wiki/Arabic_mathematics "Arabic mathematics") developed [spherical trigonometry](/wiki/Spherical_trigonometry "Spherical trigonometry");[[56]](#cite_note-David_A._King_1993-56) in the 11th century, [al-Biruni](/wiki/Abu_Rayhan_Biruni "Abu Rayhan Biruni") used it to find the direction of Mecca from many cities and published it in *The Determination of the Co-ordinates of Cities*.[[74]](#cite_note-74) This determined the [Qibla](/wiki/Qibla "Qibla"), or Muslim direction of prayer.

### Magnetic declination

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=23 "Edit section: Magnetic declination")]

[Muslim astronomers](/wiki/Islamic_astronomy "Islamic astronomy") and geographers were aware of [magnetic declination](/wiki/Magnetic_declination "Magnetic declination") by the 15th century, when the Egyptian astronomer ['Abd al-'Aziz al-Wafa'i](/wiki/%27Abd_al-%27Aziz_al-Wafa%27i "'Abd al-'Aziz al-Wafa'i") (d. 1469/1471) measured it as 7 degrees from [Cairo](/wiki/Cairo "Cairo").[[75]](#cite_note-75)

## Medieval Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Medieval Europe")]

### Greek influence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Greek influence")]

In medieval Europe, knowledge of the sphericity of Earth survived into the medieval corpus of knowledge by direct transmission of the texts of Greek antiquity ([Aristotle](/wiki/Aristotle "Aristotle")), and via authors such as [Isidore of Seville](/wiki/Isidore_of_Seville "Isidore of Seville") and the [Venerable Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede"). It became increasingly traceable with the rise of [scholasticism](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism") and [medieval learning](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university").[[37]](#cite_note-Reinhard_Krüger-37)

Revising the figures attributed to Posidonius, another Greek philosopher determined 18,000 miles (29,000 km) as the Earth's circumference. This last figure was promulgated by [Ptolemy](/wiki/Ptolemy "Ptolemy") through his world maps. The maps of Ptolemy strongly influenced the cartographers of the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). It is probable that [Christopher Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus"), using such maps, was led to believe that Asia was only 3,000 or 4,000 miles (4,800 or 6,400 km) west of Europe.[[76]](#cite_note-76)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

Ptolemy's view was not universal, however, and chapter 20 of [Sir John Mandeville](/wiki/Sir_John_Mandeville "Sir John Mandeville")'s *Travels* (c. 1357) supports Eratosthenes' calculation.

Spread of this knowledge beyond the immediate sphere of Greco-Roman scholarship was necessarily gradual, associated with the pace of [Christianisation](/wiki/Christianisation "Christianisation") of Europe. For example, the first evidence of knowledge of the spherical shape of Earth in [Scandinavia](/wiki/Scandinavia "Scandinavia") is a 12th-century [Old Icelandic](/wiki/Old_Icelandic "Old Icelandic") translation of *[Elucidarius](/wiki/Elucidarius "Elucidarius")*.[[77]](#cite_note-77) A list of more than a hundred [Latin](/wiki/Latin "Latin") and vernacular writers from [Late Antiquity](/wiki/Late_Antiquity "Late Antiquity") and the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages") who were aware that Earth was spherical has been compiled by Reinhard Krüger, professor for Romance literature at the [University of Stuttgart](/wiki/University_of_Stuttgart "University of Stuttgart").[[37]](#cite_note-Reinhard_Krüger-37)

It was not until the 16th century that his concept of the Earth's size was revised. During that period the Flemish cartographer, [Mercator](/wiki/Gerardus_Mercator "Gerardus Mercator"), made successive reductions in the size of the [Mediterranean Sea](/wiki/Mediterranean_Sea "Mediterranean Sea") and all of Europe which had the effect of increasing the size of the Earth.

### Early medieval Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Early medieval Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/78/Hildegard_von_Bingen-_%27Werk_Gottes%27%2C_12._Jh..jpg/250px-Hildegard_von_Bingen-_%27Werk_Gottes%27%2C_12._Jh..jpg)](/wiki/File:Hildegard_von_Bingen-_%27Werk_Gottes%27,_12._Jh..jpg)

Spherical Earth with the four seasons. Illustration in 12th-century book *Liber Divinorum Operum* by [Hildegard of Bingen](/wiki/Hildegard_of_Bingen "Hildegard of Bingen")

#### Isidore of Seville

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Isidore of Seville")]

Bishop [Isidore of Seville](/wiki/Isidore_of_Seville "Isidore of Seville") (560–636) taught in his widely read encyclopedia, *The [Etymologies](/wiki/Etymologiae "Etymologiae")*, that Earth was "round".[[78]](#cite_note-78) The bishop's confusing exposition and choice of imprecise Latin terms have divided scholarly opinion on whether he meant a sphere or a disk or even whether he meant anything specific.[[79]](#cite_note-79) Notable recent scholars claim that he taught a spherical Earth.[[80]](#cite_note-80) Isidore did not admit the possibility of people dwelling at the antipodes, considering them as legendary[[81]](#cite_note-81) and noting that there was no evidence for their existence.[[82]](#cite_note-82)

#### Bede the Venerable

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Bede the Venerable")]

The monk [Bede](/wiki/Bede "Bede") (c. 672–735) wrote in his influential treatise on [computus](/wiki/Computus "Computus"), *The Reckoning of Time*, that Earth was round. He explained the unequal length of daylight from "the roundness of the Earth, for not without reason is it called 'the orb of the world' on the pages of Holy Scripture and of ordinary literature. It is, in fact, set like a sphere in the middle of the whole universe."[[83]](#cite_note-83) The large number of surviving manuscripts of *The Reckoning of Time*, copied to meet the Carolingian requirement that all priests should study the computus, indicates that many, if not most, priests were exposed to the idea of the sphericity of Earth.[[84]](#cite_note-84) [Ælfric of Eynsham](/wiki/%C3%86lfric_of_Eynsham "Ælfric of Eynsham") paraphrased Bede into Old English, saying, "Now the Earth's roundness and the Sun's orbit constitute the obstacle to the day's being equally long in every land."[[85]](#cite_note-85)

Bede was lucid about Earth's sphericity, writing "We call the earth a globe, not as if the shape of a sphere were expressed in the diversity of plains and mountains, but because, if all things are included in the outline, the earth's circumference will represent the figure of a perfect globe ... For truly it is an orb placed in the centre of the universe; in its width it is like a circle, and not circular like a shield but rather like a ball, and it extends from its centre with perfect roundness on all sides."[[86]](#cite_note-Russell-86)

#### Anania Shirakatsi

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Anania Shirakatsi")]

The 7th-century [Armenian](/wiki/Armenians "Armenians") scholar [Anania Shirakatsi](/wiki/Anania_Shirakatsi "Anania Shirakatsi") described the world as "being like an egg with a spherical yolk (the globe) surrounded by a layer of white (the atmosphere) and covered with a hard shell (the sky)".[[87]](#cite_note-87)

### High and late medieval Europe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=30 "Edit section: High and late medieval Europe")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/f/f1/John_Gower_world_Vox_Clamantis.jpg/330px-John_Gower_world_Vox_Clamantis.jpg)](/wiki/File:John_Gower_world_Vox_Clamantis.jpg)

[John Gower](/wiki/John_Gower "John Gower") prepares to shoot the world, a sphere with compartments representing earth, air, and water (from his *[Vox Clamantis](/wiki/Vox_Clamantis "Vox Clamantis")*, around 1400)

During the [High Middle Ages](/wiki/High_Middle_Ages "High Middle Ages"), the astronomical knowledge in Christian Europe was extended beyond what was transmitted directly from ancient authors by transmission of learning from [medieval Islamic astronomy](/wiki/Medieval_Islamic_astronomy "Medieval Islamic astronomy"). An early student of such learning was Gerbert d'Aurillac, the later [Pope Sylvester II](/wiki/Pope_Sylvester_II "Pope Sylvester II").

Saint Hildegard ([Hildegard von Bingen](/wiki/Hildegard_von_Bingen "Hildegard von Bingen"), 1098–1179), depicted the spherical Earth several times in her work *Liber Divinorum Operum*.[[88]](#cite_note-88)

[Johannes de Sacrobosco](/wiki/Johannes_de_Sacrobosco "Johannes de Sacrobosco") (c. 1195 – c. 1256 AD) wrote a famous work on Astronomy called *[Tractatus de Sphaera](/wiki/Tractatus_de_Sphaera "Tractatus de Sphaera")*, based on Ptolemy, which primarily considers the sphere of the sky. However, it contains clear proofs of Earth's sphericity in the first chapter.[[89]](#cite_note-89)[[90]](#cite_note-90)

Many [scholastic](/wiki/Scholasticism "Scholasticism") commentators on Aristotle's *[On the Heavens](/wiki/On_the_Heavens "On the Heavens")* and Sacrobosco's *Treatise on the Sphere* unanimously agreed that Earth is spherical or round.[[91]](#cite_note-91) Grant observes that no author who had studied at a [medieval university](/wiki/Medieval_university "Medieval university") thought that Earth was flat.[[92]](#cite_note-92)

The *Elucidarium* of [Honorius Augustodunensis](/wiki/Honorius_Augustodunensis "Honorius Augustodunensis") (c. 1120), an important manual for the instruction of lesser clergy, which was translated into [Middle English](/wiki/Middle_English "Middle English"), [Old French](/wiki/Old_French "Old French"), [Middle High German](/wiki/Middle_High_German "Middle High German"), [Old Russian](/wiki/Old_Russian "Old Russian"), [Middle Dutch](/wiki/Middle_Dutch "Middle Dutch"), [Old Norse](/wiki/Old_Norse "Old Norse"), [Icelandic](/wiki/Icelandic_language "Icelandic language"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_language "Spanish language"), and several Italian dialects, explicitly refers to a spherical Earth.[[93]](#cite_note-93) Likewise, the fact that [Bertold von Regensburg](/wiki/Bertold_von_Regensburg "Bertold von Regensburg") (mid-13th century) used the spherical Earth as an illustration in a [sermon](/wiki/Sermon "Sermon") shows that he could assume this knowledge among his congregation. The sermon was preached in the vernacular German, and thus was not intended for a learned audience.

Dante's *[Divine Comedy](/wiki/Divine_Comedy "Divine Comedy")*, written in Italian in the early 14th century, portrays Earth as a sphere, discussing implications such as the different stars visible in the [Southern Hemisphere](/wiki/Southern_Hemisphere "Southern Hemisphere"), the altered position of the Sun, and the various [time zones](/wiki/Time_zone "Time zone") of Earth.

## Early modern period

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=31 "Edit section: Early modern period")]

The invention of the [telescope](/wiki/Telescope "Telescope") and the [theodolite](/wiki/Theodolite "Theodolite") and the development of [logarithm tables](/wiki/Logarithm_table "Logarithm table") allowed exact [triangulation](/wiki/Triangulation_(surveying) "Triangulation (surveying)") and [arc measurements](/wiki/Arc_measurement "Arc measurement").

### Ming China

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=32 "Edit section: Ming China")]

[Joseph Needham](/wiki/Joseph_Needham "Joseph Needham"), in his *Chinese Cosmology* reports that [Shen Kuo](/wiki/Shen_Kuo "Shen Kuo") (1031–1095) used models of lunar eclipse and solar eclipse to conclude the roundness of celestial bodies.[[94]](#cite_note-:0-94)

> If they were like balls they would surely obstruct each other when they met. I replied that these celestial bodies were certainly like balls. How do we know this? By the waxing and waning of the moon. The moon itself gives forth no light, but is like a ball of silver; the light is the light of the sun (reflected). When the brightness is first seen, the sun (-light passes almost) alongside, so the side only is illuminated and looks like a crescent. When the sun gradually gets further away, the light shines slanting, and the moon is full, round like a bullet. If half of a sphere is covered with (white) powder and looked at from the side, the covered part will look like a crescent; if looked at from the front, it will appear round. Thus we know that the celestial bodies are spherical.

However, Shen's ideas did not gain widespread acceptance or consideration, as the shape of Earth was not important to Confucian officials who were more concerned with human relations.[[94]](#cite_note-:0-94) In the 17th century, the idea of a spherical Earth, now considerably advanced by [Western astronomy](/wiki/Scientific_revolution "Scientific revolution"), ultimately spread to [Ming China](/wiki/Ming_China "Ming China"), when [Jesuit missionaries](/wiki/Jesuit_China_missions "Jesuit China missions"), who held high positions as astronomers at the imperial court, successfully challenged the Chinese belief that Earth was flat and square.[[95]](#cite_note-Jean-Claude_Martzloff_69-95)[[96]](#cite_note-Christopher_Cullen_(1980)-96)[[97]](#cite_note-Christopher_Cullen_(1976)-97)

The *Ge zhi cao* (格致草) treatise of Xiong Mingyu (熊明遇) published in 1648 showed a printed picture of Earth as a spherical globe, with the text stating that "the round Earth certainly has no square corners".[[98]](#cite_note-needham_volume_3_499-98) The text also pointed out that sailing ships could return to their port of origin after circumnavigating the waters of Earth.[[98]](#cite_note-needham_volume_3_499-98)

The influence of the map is distinctly Western, as traditional maps of Chinese cartography held the graduation of the sphere at 365.25 degrees, while the Western graduation was of 360 degrees.[[98]](#cite_note-needham_volume_3_499-98)[*[relevant?](/wiki/Wikipedia:Writing_better_articles#Stay_on_topic "Wikipedia:Writing better articles")*] The adoption of European astronomy, facilitated by the failure of indigenous astronomy to make progress, was accompanied by a [sinocentric](/wiki/Sinocentric "Sinocentric") reinterpretation that declared the imported ideas Chinese in origin:

> European astronomy was so much judged worth consideration that numerous Chinese authors developed the idea that the Chinese of antiquity had anticipated most of the novelties presented by the missionaries as European discoveries, for example, the rotundity of the Earth and the "heavenly spherical star carrier model". Making skillful use of philology, these authors cleverly reinterpreted the greatest technical and literary works of Chinese antiquity. From this sprang a new science wholly dedicated to the demonstration of the Chinese origin of astronomy and more generally of all European science and technology.[[95]](#cite_note-Jean-Claude_Martzloff_69-95)

Although mainstream Chinese science until the 17th century held the view that Earth was flat, square, and enveloped by the [celestial sphere](/wiki/Celestial_sphere "Celestial sphere"), this idea was criticized by the [Jin-dynasty](/wiki/Jin_dynasty_(265%E2%80%93420) "Jin dynasty (265–420)") scholar [Yu Xi](/wiki/Yu_Xi "Yu Xi") (fl. 307–345), who suggested that Earth could be either square or round, in accordance with the shape of the heavens.[[99]](#cite_note-99) The [Yuan-dynasty](/wiki/Yuan_dynasty "Yuan dynasty") mathematician [Li Ye](/wiki/Li_Ye_(mathematician) "Li Ye (mathematician)") (c. 1192–1279) firmly argued that Earth was spherical, just like the shape of the heavens only smaller, since a square Earth would hinder the movement of the heavens and celestial bodies in his estimation.[[100]](#cite_note-100) The 17th-century *Ge zhi cao* treatise also used the same terminology to describe the shape of Earth that the [Eastern-Han](/wiki/Eastern_Han_dynasty "Eastern Han dynasty") scholar [Zhang Heng](/wiki/Zhang_Heng "Zhang Heng") (78–139 AD) had used to describe the shape of the Sun and Moon (as in, that the former was as round as a [crossbow](/wiki/Crossbow "Crossbow") bullet, and the latter was the shape of a ball).[[101]](#cite_note-101)

### Circumnavigation of the globe

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=33 "Edit section: Circumnavigation of the globe")]

Further information: [Age of Discovery](/wiki/Age_of_Discovery "Age of Discovery")

The [Portuguese](/wiki/Portugal "Portugal") exploration of [Africa](/wiki/Africa "Africa") and [Asia](/wiki/Asia "Asia"), and [Columbus](/wiki/Christopher_Columbus "Christopher Columbus")'s voyage to the [Americas](/wiki/Americas "Americas") (1492) provided more direct evidence of the size and shape of the world.

The first direct demonstration of Earth's sphericity came in the form of the [first circumnavigation in history](/wiki/Magellan_expedition "Magellan expedition"), an expedition captained by Portuguese explorer [Ferdinand Magellan](/wiki/Ferdinand_Magellan "Ferdinand Magellan").[[102]](#cite_note-102) The expedition was financed by the Spanish Crown. On August 10, 1519, the five ships under Magellan's command departed from [Seville](/wiki/Seville "Seville"). They crossed the [Atlantic Ocean](/wiki/Atlantic_Ocean "Atlantic Ocean"), passed through what is now called the [Strait of Magellan](/wiki/Strait_of_Magellan "Strait of Magellan"), crossed the Pacific, and arrived in [Cebu](/wiki/Cebu "Cebu"), where Magellan was killed by Philippine natives in a battle. His second in command, the Spaniard [Juan Sebastián Elcano](/wiki/Juan_Sebasti%C3%A1n_Elcano "Juan Sebastián Elcano"), continued the expedition and, on September 6, 1522, arrived at Seville, completing the circumnavigation. [Charles I of Spain](/wiki/Charles_I_of_Spain "Charles I of Spain"), in recognition of his feat, gave Elcano a [coat of arms](/wiki/Coat_of_arms "Coat of arms") with the motto *Primus circumdedisti me* (in Latin, "You went around me first").[[103]](#cite_note-103)

A circumnavigation alone does not prove that Earth is spherical: it could be cylindric, irregularly globular, or one of many other shapes. Still, combined with trigonometric evidence of the form used by Eratosthenes 1,700 years prior, the Magellan expedition removed any reasonable doubt in educated circles in Europe.[[104]](#cite_note-104) The [Transglobe Expedition](/wiki/Transglobe_Expedition "Transglobe Expedition") (1979–1982) was the first expedition to make a circumpolar circumnavigation, travelling the world "vertically" traversing both of the poles of rotation using only surface transport.

### European calculations

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=34 "Edit section: European calculations")]

In the [Carolingian era](/wiki/Carolingian_Renaissance "Carolingian Renaissance"), scholars discussed Macrobius's view of the *[antipodes](/wiki/Antipodes "Antipodes")*. One of them, the Irish monk [Dungal](/wiki/Saint_Dungal "Saint Dungal"), asserted that the tropical gap between our habitable region and the other habitable region to the south was smaller than Macrobius had believed.[[105]](#cite_note-105)

In 1505 the cosmographer and explorer [Duarte Pacheco Pereira](/wiki/Duarte_Pacheco_Pereira "Duarte Pacheco Pereira") calculated the value of the degree of the [meridian arc](/wiki/Meridian_arc "Meridian arc") with a margin of error of only 4%, when the current error at the time varied between 7 and 15%.[[106]](#cite_note-106)

[Jean Picard](/wiki/Jean_Picard "Jean Picard") performed the first modern meridian arc measurement in 1669–1670. He measured a [baseline](/wiki/Baseline_(surveying) "Baseline (surveying)") using wooden rods, a telescope (for his [angular measurements](/wiki/Angle#Angles_in_geography_and_astronomy "Angle")), and [logarithms](/wiki/Logarithms "Logarithms") (for computation). [Gian Domenico Cassini](/wiki/Giovanni_Domenico_Cassini "Giovanni Domenico Cassini") then his son [Jacques Cassini](/wiki/Jacques_Cassini "Jacques Cassini") later continued Picard's arc ([Paris meridian](/wiki/Paris_meridian "Paris meridian") arc) northward to [Dunkirk](/wiki/Dunkirk,_France "Dunkirk, France") and southward to the [Spanish border](/wiki/France%E2%80%93Spain_border "France–Spain border"). Cassini divided the measured arc into two parts, one northward from [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris"), another southward. When he computed the length of a degree from both chains, he found that the length of one degree of [latitude](/wiki/Latitude "Latitude") in the northern part of the chain was shorter than that in the southern part (see illustration).

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/58/CASSINIS%27_ELLIPSOID%3B_HUYGEN%27S_THEORETICAL_ELLIPSOID.GIF/250px-CASSINIS%27_ELLIPSOID%3B_HUYGEN%27S_THEORETICAL_ELLIPSOID.GIF)](/wiki/File:CASSINIS%27_ELLIPSOID;_HUYGEN%27S_THEORETICAL_ELLIPSOID.GIF)

Cassini's ellipsoid; Huygens' theoretical ellipsoid

This result, if correct, meant that Earth was not a sphere, but a [prolate spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid") (taller than wide). However, this contradicted computations by [Isaac Newton](/wiki/Isaac_Newton "Isaac Newton") and [Christiaan Huygens](/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens "Christiaan Huygens"). In 1659, [Christiaan Huygens](/wiki/Christiaan_Huygens "Christiaan Huygens") was the first to derive the now standard formula for the [centrifugal force](/wiki/Centrifugal_force "Centrifugal force") in his work *De vi centrifuga*. The formula played a central role in [classical mechanics](/wiki/Classical_mechanics "Classical mechanics") and became known as the second of [Newton's laws of motion](/wiki/Newton%27s_laws_of_motion "Newton's laws of motion"). Newton's [theory of gravitation](/wiki/Law_of_universal_gravitation "Law of universal gravitation") combined with the rotation of the Earth predicted the Earth to be an [oblate spheroid](/wiki/Spheroid "Spheroid") (wider than tall), with a [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") of 1:230.[[107]](#cite_note-107)

The issue could be settled by measuring, for a number of points on Earth, the relationship between their distance (in north–south direction) and the angles between their [zeniths](/wiki/Zenith "Zenith"). On an oblate Earth, the [meridional](/wiki/Meridional "Meridional") [distance corresponding to one degree of latitude](/wiki/Meridian_arc "Meridian arc") will grow toward the poles, as can be [demonstrated mathematically](/wiki/Meridian_arc#Meridian_distance_on_the_ellipsoid "Meridian arc").

The [French Academy of Sciences](/wiki/French_Academy_of_Sciences "French Academy of Sciences") dispatched two expeditions. One expedition (1736–37) under [Pierre Louis Maupertuis](/wiki/Pierre_Louis_Maupertuis "Pierre Louis Maupertuis") was sent to [Torne Valley](/wiki/Me%C3%A4nmaa#French_Geodesic_Mission "Meänmaa") (near the Earth's North Pole). The [second mission](/wiki/French_Geodesic_Mission "French Geodesic Mission") (1735–1744) under [Pierre Bouguer](/wiki/Pierre_Bouguer "Pierre Bouguer") was sent to what is modern-day [Ecuador](/wiki/Ecuador "Ecuador"), near the equator. Their measurements demonstrated an oblate Earth, with a flattening of 1:210. This approximation to the true shape of the Earth became the new [reference ellipsoid](/wiki/Reference_ellipsoid "Reference ellipsoid").

In 1787 the first precise trigonometric survey to be undertaken within Britain was the [Anglo-French Survey](/wiki/Anglo-French_Survey_(1784%E2%80%931790) "Anglo-French Survey (1784–1790)"). Its purpose was to link the Greenwich and Paris' observatories.[[108]](#cite_note-108) The survey is very significant as the forerunner of the work of the [Ordnance Survey](/wiki/Ordnance_Survey "Ordnance Survey") which was founded in 1791, one year after [William Roy](/wiki/William_Roy "William Roy")'s death.

[Johann Georg Tralles](/wiki/Johann_Georg_Tralles "Johann Georg Tralles") surveyed the [Bernese Oberland](/wiki/Bernese_Oberland "Bernese Oberland"), then the entire [Canton of Bern](/wiki/Canton_of_Bern "Canton of Bern"). Soon after the Anglo-French Survey, in 1791 and 1797, he and his pupil [Ferdinand Rudolph Hassler](/wiki/Ferdinand_Rudolph_Hassler "Ferdinand Rudolph Hassler") measured the base of the [Grand Marais](/wiki/Grand_Marais,_Seeland "Grand Marais, Seeland") (German: *Grosses Moos*) near [Aarberg](/wiki/Aarberg "Aarberg") in [Seeland](/wiki/Seeland_(administrative_district) "Seeland (administrative district)"). This work earned Tralles to be appointed as the representative of the [Helvetic Republic](/wiki/Helvetic_Republic "Helvetic Republic") on the international scientific committee meeting in Paris from 1798 to 1799 to determine the length of the *[metre](/wiki/Metre "Metre")*.[[109]](#cite_note-:6-109)[[110]](#cite_note-110)[[111]](#cite_note-111)[[112]](#cite_note-112)

The [French Academy of Sciences](/wiki/French_Academy_of_Sciences "French Academy of Sciences") had commissioned an expedition led by [Jean Baptiste Joseph Delambre](/wiki/Jean_Baptiste_Joseph_Delambre "Jean Baptiste Joseph Delambre") and [Pierre Méchain](/wiki/Pierre_M%C3%A9chain "Pierre Méchain"), lasting from 1792 to 1799, which attempted to accurately measure the distance between a belfry in [Dunkerque](/wiki/Dunkirk "Dunkirk") and [Montjuïc castle](/wiki/Montju%C3%AFc_Castle_(Barcelona) "Montjuïc Castle (Barcelona)") in [Barcelona](/wiki/Barcelona "Barcelona") at the [longitude](/wiki/Longitude "Longitude") of [Paris Panthéon](/wiki/Panth%C3%A9on "Panthéon"). The *metre* was defined as one ten-millionth of the shortest distance from the North Pole to the equator [passing through Paris](/wiki/Paris_meridian "Paris meridian"), assuming an Earth's [flattening](/wiki/Flattening "Flattening") of 1/334. The committee extrapolated from Delambre and Méchain's survey the distance from the [North Pole](/wiki/North_Pole "North Pole") to the [Equator](/wiki/Equator "Equator") which was 5 130 740 [toises](/wiki/Toise "Toise"). As the [metre](/wiki/Metre "Metre") had to be equal to one ten-million of this distance, it was defined as 0,513074 toises or 443,296 [lignes](/wiki/Ligne "Ligne") of the Toise of Peru (see below).[[113]](#cite_note-113)[[114]](#cite_note-114)[[115]](#cite_note-Delambre-115)[[116]](#cite_note-116)

### Asia and Americas

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=35 "Edit section: Asia and Americas")]

A discovery made in 1672–1673 by [Jean Richer](/wiki/Jean_Richer "Jean Richer") turned the attention of mathematicians to the deviation of the [Earth's shape](/wiki/Figure_of_the_Earth "Figure of the Earth") from a spherical form. This astronomer, having been sent by the [Academy of Sciences of Paris](/wiki/French_Academy_of_Sciences "French Academy of Sciences") to [Cayenne](/wiki/Cayenne "Cayenne"), in South America, for the purpose of investigating the amount of [astronomical refraction](/wiki/Atmospheric_refraction "Atmospheric refraction") and other astronomical objects, notably the [parallax](/wiki/Parallax "Parallax") of [Mars](/wiki/Mars "Mars") between [Paris](/wiki/Paris "Paris") and Cayenne in order to determine the [Earth-Sun distance](/wiki/Astronomical_unit "Astronomical unit"), observed that his clock, which had been regulated at Paris to beat seconds, lost about two minutes and a half daily at Cayenne, and that in order to bring it to measure mean solar time it was necessary to shorten the [pendulum](/wiki/Pendulum "Pendulum") by more than a line (about 1⁄12th of an in.). This fact was scarcely credited till it had been confirmed by the subsequent observations of Varin and Deshayes on the coasts of Africa and America.[[117]](#cite_note-:1-117)[[118]](#cite_note-118)

In [South America](/wiki/South_America "South America") Bouguer noticed, as did [George Everest](/wiki/George_Everest "George Everest") in the 19th century [Great Trigonometric Survey](/wiki/Great_Trigonometric_Survey "Great Trigonometric Survey") of India, that the astronomical vertical tended to be pulled in the direction of large mountain ranges, due to the [gravitational](/wiki/Gravitation "Gravitation") attraction of these huge piles of rock. As this vertical is everywhere perpendicular to the idealized surface of mean sea level, or the [geoid](/wiki/Geoid "Geoid"), this means that the figure of the Earth is even more irregular than an ellipsoid of revolution. Thus the study of the "[undulation of the geoid](/wiki/Undulation_of_the_geoid "Undulation of the geoid")" became the next great undertaking in the science of studying the figure of the Earth.

## 19th century

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=History_of_geodesy&action=edit&section=36 "Edit section: 19th century")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/38/Litography_archive_of_the_Bayerisches_Vermessungsamt.jpg/250px-Litography_archive_of_the_Bayerisches_Vermessungsamt.jpg)](/wiki/File:Litography_archive_of_the_Bayerisches_Vermessungsamt.jpg)

Archive with [lithography](/wiki/Lithography "Lithography") plates for maps of [Bavaria](/wiki/Bavaria "Bavaria") in the *Landesamt für Vermessung und Geoinformation* in [Munich](/wiki/Munich "Munich")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/5/59/Litography_negative_stone_and_positive_paper.jpg/330px-Litography_negative_stone_and_positive_paper.jpg)](/wiki/File:Litography_negative_stone_and_positive_paper.jpg)

Negative lithography stone and positive print of a historic map of Munich

In the late 19th century the [Mitteleuropäische Gradmessung](/wiki/International_Association_of_Geodesy "International Association of Geodesy") (Central European Arc Measurement) was established by several central European countries and a Central Bureau was set up at the expense of [Prussia](/wiki/Prussia "Prussia"), within the Geodetic Institute at Berlin.[[119]](#cite_note-119) One of its most important goals was the derivation of an international [ellipsoid](/wiki/Ellipsoid "Ellipsoid") and a [gravity](/wiki/Gravity "Gravity") formula which should be optimal not only for [Europe](/wiki/Europe "Europe") but also for the whole world. The [Mitteleuropäische Gradmessung](/wiki/International_Association_of_Geodesy "International Association of Geodesy") was an early predecessor of the [International Association of Geodesy](/wiki/International_Association_of_Geodesy "International Association of Geodesy") (IAG) one of the constituent sections of the [International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics](/wiki/International_Union_of_Geodesy_and_Geophysics "International Union of Geodesy and Geophysics") (IUGG) which was founded in 1919.[[120]](#cite_note-120)[[121]](#cite_note-121)

### Prime meridian and standard of length
